title: Check yourself: the skeleton
questions.0.q: You put a heading inside <head> instead of <body>. What appears on screen?
questions.0.options.3: The heading, but up in the tab
questions.0.explain: <head> holds information ABOUT the page, not content OF the page. Whatever you put there, the person will not see it — no matter how correctly it is written.
questions.1.q: A friend says: "my page works without <html> and <body>, so they are not needed". Is he right?
questions.1.options.0: Yes — if it works, it is unnecessary
questions.1.options.1: No — the browser silently adds them for him
questions.1.options.2: Yes, but only for simple pages
questions.1.options.3: No — the page does not actually work, he is deceiving himself
questions.1.explain: The browser is forgiving: seeing bare text, it silently wraps it and shows it. So the page works not because of him, but despite him. It is dangerous to rely on someone else's forgiveness.
questions.2.q: Where does the text from <title> go?
questions.2.options.0: To the top of the page, as a big heading
questions.2.options.1: To the tab label, to bookmarks and to Google
questions.2.options.2: Nowhere — it is only for tidying the code
questions.2.explain: That is why <title> is the first thing a stranger sees about your page — before they even open it. A lazy <title> means nobody will open it.
questions.3.q: You delete <!DOCTYPE html> and the page looks exactly the same. What is the conclusion?
questions.3.options.0: That it is pointless and can be skipped
questions.3.options.1: That the page is still too simple to break
questions.3.options.2: That your browser is newer than others
questions.3.options.3: That you wrote the rest perfectly
questions.3.explain: No symptom does not mean no problem. This is perhaps the most useful thing you will learn in the entire course — and it applies far beyond HTML.
